summary: In patients with Eisenmenger syndrome, there is marked dilation of the central pulmonary arteries secondary to chronic hypoxemia-induced vascular remodelling processes; while peripheral arterial pruning occurs too but isn't universally prominent enough for primary diagnostic significance compared to central vessels' notable enlargement. Central pulmonic artery dilatation stands out more definitively among typical findings associated directly linked causally back towards core pathophysiology driving condition progression. Peripheral pruned appearance may occur secondarily through compensatory mechanisms yet lacks same level specificity seen centrally within main trunk structures affected first chronologically during disease evolution timeline progressing toward severe forms like full-blown ESMR where such morphological shifts become most pronounced clinically observable features guiding diagnosis & management strategies employed therapeutically targeting underlying hemodynamic derangements causing irreversible lung damage leading ultimately fatal outcomes if left untreated over prolonged periods without intervention measures put into place timely manner preventing further deterioration once recognized early stages present themselves symptomatically evident via echocardiography imaging studies confirming presence/absence thereof accurately diagnosing patient status at given point time course illness trajectory being mapped out longitudinally throughout follow-up visits monitoring progress
